title: The 'I Should Know This' Vault
description: Common knowledge and basic facts that often become difficult to recall under the pressure of a timer.

example.question: Which human finger is the only one to contain two phalanges instead of three?
example.clarity_note: Phalanges are the digital bones of the hands and feet.

title: Slogans & Jingles
description: Identifying famous brands based on their iconic catchphrases or short musical signatures.

example.question: Which snack brand uses the tagline 'The snack that smiles back'?

title: Movie 'Before They Were Famous'
description: Identifying A-list actors based on their early, minor, or obscure roles before they achieved stardom.

example.question: Which 'Friends' star made their film debut in the 1993 horror movie 'Leprechaun'?
example.clarity_note: She played the character Tory Reding.

title: Badly Explained Plots
description: Summarizing famous movies or books using the most technically correct but misleading descriptions possible.

example.question: A grumpy green guy and a talking donkey travel to save a princess so the guy can get his yard back.

title: Foodie Geography
description: Challenging the true geographical origins of famous dishes, ingredients, and culinary traditions.

example.question: In which country were 'French Fries' actually invented?
example.clarity_note: The name 'French' refers to the way the potatoes are sliced (julienned).

title: One-Hit Wonders
description: Naming the artist or group behind a massively popular song that was their only major chart success.

example.question: Who performed the 1990 hit 'Ice Ice Baby'?

title: Tech & Toys of the Decades
description: A nostalgia-driven category focused on gadgets, games, and playthings from the 80s, 90s, and 2000s.

example.question: What was the name of the handheld digital pet that became a global craze in 1996?
example.clarity_note: The name is a portmanteau of the Japanese words for 'egg' and 'watch'.

title: Animal Kingdom Superlatives
description: Questions about the fastest, largest, loudest, and most extreme creatures in the natural world.

example.question: Which animal has the largest eyes in the world, roughly the size of a dinner plate?
example.clarity_note: Their eyes can reach up to 27cm (11 inches) in diameter.

title: Real or Fake?
description: Teams must distinguish between a bizarre true fact and a total fabrication from a list of options.

example.question: Which of the following is a TRUE historical fact?
example.answer: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire
distractors: The Eiffel Tower was originally built for London
example.clarity_note: Teaching existed at Oxford as early as 1096; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1325.

title: Literal Lyrics
description: Famous song lyrics translated into overly formal, academic, or clinical English prose.

example.question: Identify the song: 'Please cease your movements, for it is currently the period designated for the use of a percussion instrument.'

title: The 1-Star Review
description: Identifying famous movies, books, or landmarks based on humorous, overly critical 1-star internet reviews.

example.question: Which movie was reviewed as: 'Way too much walking. Just a bunch of guys complaining about a piece of jewelry.'?

title: Emoji Cryptograms
description: Deciphering movie titles, songs, or famous phrases represented entirely by a string of emojis.

example.question: Decipher this movie title: 🦁👑

title: Historical 'Which Came First?'
description: Comparing the timelines of historical events or inventions that people often don't realize overlapped.

example.question: Which of these was established or invented FIRST?
example.clarity_note: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company.

title: The Stage Name Shuffle
description: Providing the legal birth name of a celebrity and requiring the player to identify their famous stage name.

example.question: What is the stage name of Stefani Joanne Angelina Germanotta?

title: Logo Logic
description: Exploring the hidden symbols, negative space, and clever meanings tucked into world-famous brand logos.

example.question: What 'hidden' image is found in the negative space of the FedEx logo?
example.clarity_note: It is located between the 'E' and the 'x'.

title: Strange Laws: Real or Ridiculous?
description: A multiple-choice challenge to identify which bizarre legal restriction is actually a real law.

example.question: In Gainesville, Georgia, it is technically illegal to eat fried chicken with what?
example.clarity_note: Passed in 1961 as a PR stunt to promote Gainesville as the poultry capital.

title: Pop Culture Math
description: Solving simple arithmetic problems where the numbers are derived from famous movies, bands, or facts.

example.question: (Number of Dwarves in Snow White) + (Number of Strings on a standard Cello) = ?

title: Fictional Corporate Giants
description: Matching fictional companies, products, or brands to the TV shows or movies in which they appear.

example.question: Which company serves as the primary employer in the US version of 'The Office'?
example.clarity_note: Wernham Hogg is the company in the original UK version.

title: Urban Legend Debunkers
description: Sorting through common misconceptions and 'facts' to find the one that is scientifically or historically true.

example.question: Which of these widely cited 'facts' is actually scientifically true?
distractors: You swallow 8 spiders a year in your sleep
Humans only use 10% of their brains
example.clarity_note: Bananas grow on herbaceous plants, not trees.

title: Misheard Lyric Madness
description: We provide a famous misheard lyric (Mondegreen), and you must identify the correct song or actual lyric.

example.question: If the misheard lyric is 'Excuse me while I kiss this guy!', what is the actual lyric?
example.answer: Excuse me while I kiss the sky
distractors: Excuse me while I miss the pie
Excuse me while I hit the guy
Excuse me while I kiss this fly
example.clarity_note: From the song 'Purple Haze' by Jimi Hendrix.
